coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/ polyvinyl alcohol nanocomposite for photon management: Application in CIGS photovoltaic cells
coredata.dc:creator: Alshahrani, Thamraa
Khan, Firoz
Al-Rasheidi, Masoud
Fareed, Imran
Majid, M. Abdul
Kim, Jae Hyun
coredata.dc:description:
The efficiency (η) of copper indium gallium selenide (CIGS) solar cells has been hampered by absorption losses that happen within the ZnO and CdS layers. A photon downconversion layer created from polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) composite (NGQDs/PVA) was implemented on the top of the cell to overcome this obstacle. Notably, the NGQDs/PVA layers, prepared at spin speeds of 3000, 4000, and 5000 rpm, exhibited optimal characteristics, demonstrating superior light absorption at low wavelengths along with elevated transmittance at longer wavelengths. The η was found to be highest for the layer prepared at 4000 rpm, accompanied by elevated values of short-circuit current (J
sc ), open-circuit voltage (V
oc ), and fill factor (FF). Consequently, Cell #2 incorporating the champion NGQDs/PVA layer outperformed all other CIGS photovoltaic cells, showcasing an impressive enhancement of 8.96 % in J
sc , 1.62 % in V
oc , 2.03 % in FF, and an overall η of 11.51 %. This champion cell significantly improves the performance of CIGS photovoltaics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CIGS PV cells
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Photon down converting layer
Polyvinyl alcohol

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the Saudi Standards, Metrology and Quality Organization (SASO) through the Research Program (Grant No#3-6-12). The researchers thank for the Saudi Standards, Metrology and Quality Organization (SASO) and Deanship of Scientific Research at Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University to supervise and follow up the project.

This research was funded by the Saudi Standards, Metrology and Quality Organization (SASO) through the Research Program (Grant No. 3-6-12). The researchers thank for the Saudi Standards, Metrology and Quality Organization (SASO) and Deanship of Scientific Research at Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University to supervise and follow up the project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic for (a) the synthesis of NGQDs via thermal process and (b) the formation of an LDC layer using NGQDs and PVA.
Atomic force microscopy image of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots.
(a) Optical pictures in white and UV light and (b) absorption spectra of NGQDs.

(a) PL excitation spectra (for the emission wavelength of 500, 510, and 550 nm), (b) PL emission spectra for the excitation wavelength of 360–440 nm, (c) PL peak position vs. excitation wavelength, and (d) PL peak intensity vs. excitation wavelength.
(a) Photoluminescence decay spectra, (b) photoluminescence decay lifetime, and (c) average photoluminescence decay lifetime of NGQDs/PVA composite.
Optical images of Polyvinyl alcohol layer (top) and Polyvinyl alcohol layer and graphene quantum dots composite layer (bottom) in white (left) and UV (right) light.
Transmittance of PVA-NGQS layer in the wavelength range of 200–1300 nm and (b) 200–400 nm, prepared at various spin speeds.
The schematic for the structure and mechanism of PVA-NGQD-based CIGS PV cell.
J-V characteristics of the PV cells.

The comparison of performance parameters (a) J
sc , (b) V
oc , (c) FF, and (d) η of the cells.
The performance parameters enhancement of the champion cell relative to the control cell.

Analytical photovoltaic cell parameters (a) J
ph , (b) R
sh , (c) R
s , (d) n, and (e) J
0 of PV cells.
PL decay fitting parameters at various emission wavelengths.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/ polyvinyl alcohol nanocomposite for photon management: Application in CIGS photovoltaic cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The efficiency (η) of copper indium gallium selenide (CIGS) solar cells has been hampered by absorption losses that happen within the ZnO and CdS layers. A photon downconversion layer created from polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) composite (NGQDs/PVA) was implemented on the top of the cell to overcome this obstacle. Notably, the NGQDs/PVA layers, prepared at spin speeds of 3000, 4000, and 5000 rpm, exhibited optimal characteristics, demonstrating superior light absorption at low wavelengths along with elevated transmittance at longer wavelengths. The η was found to be highest for the layer prepared at 4000 rpm, accompanied by elevated values of short-circuit current (J
sc ), open-circuit voltage (V
oc ), and fill factor (FF). Consequently, Cell #2 incorporating the champion NGQDs/PVA layer outperformed all other CIGS photovoltaic cells, showcasing an impressive enhancement of 8.96 % in J
sc , 1.62 % in V
oc , 2.03 % in FF, and an overall η of 11.51 %. This champion cell significantly improves the performance of CIGS photovoltaics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently received a lot of interest for their attractive attributessuch as photoluminescence (PL) , bandgap (Eg) alteration, excellent photostability, extraordinary biocompatibility, and cost-effectiveness [1–3]. Owing to its zero-dimensional structure (0D), GQD has the ability to produce light over a wide range of wavelengths (λ) [4]. Consequently, the GQDs are widely utilized in a variety of different fields, including, optical devices [5], ecological study [6], sensing [7,8], medical [2,9], and photovoltaic (PV) cells [10–12].

Although various luminescent downconverter (LDC) materials, such as organic-inorganic QDs [24–27], dyes [28,29], GQDs [1,3,10,11,30], and rare-earth ions/complexes [31–33] are investigated. Nevertheless, these LDC materials are not accessible as downconverters for the CIGS PV cells because of some barriers (e.g., deficient PL QY, inadequate absorption, overlapping of absorption and emission spectra, low chemical/photostability, toxicity, costly, etc.) [34]. An effective method for synthesizing N-functionalized GQD (N-GQD) is established in our prior work. Additionally, the N-GQDs were used as a downconverter for the CIGS PV cell to improve its short-λ response [13,35].

To provide mechanical strength, chemical stability, and environmental protection, NGQDs require a further layer of polymer. Because merely placing them on the top surface is inadequate. The cross-linked polymer network improves both the optical as well as electrical characteristics [36]. Thus, a transparent polymer matrix is used as the host material for LDC [37,38]. Various polymers have been investigated as a matrix for the GQD, i.e., polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), polypyrrole (PPA), polyethylene glycol (PEG), polyaniline (PANI), poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate)(PEDOT:PSS), cellulose acetate (CA), poly (vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF), etc. [39]. PVA matrix was used for light emitting diode application [40], PANI in photoelectric devices [41], PVDF in engineering applications [42], PEDOT:PSS was used in self-powered piezoelectric devices [42], PPA matrix was used as an electrode for electrochemical application [43], and PEG matrix was used in the optoelectrical devices [44]. However, the host material and the luminescent species can originate further losses, i.e., (i) In luminescent systems, host materials occasionally absorb some of the incident light, which reduces the system's overall effectiveness of LDCs, (ii) The LDC material emits fewer photons than it absorbs, and (iii) re-absorption from the LDC material due to a partial overlapping of its emission as well as absorption spectrum.

The aqueous solution of graphene oxide (GO) was obtained by dispersing the powder in deionized (DI) water and then sonicating it for 5 h. There was 2 mg/ml of GO present. Using polyethyleneimine (PEI), GO was functionalized for 4 h at 90 °C by stirring the GO/PEI complex. The modified GO was subjected to heating in N2 for 2 h at 300 °C. Two hours were spent heating the samples. The NGQDs were created during the heating process. The NGQD solutions were prepared via the dispersion of NGQDs in ethanol. Fig. 1 a demonstrates the schematic of the NGQDs synthesis using GO and PEI via a thermal process in a horizontal furnace quartz tube at 300 °C. However, the formation of NGQDs/PVA is illustrated in Fig. 1b. The NGQDs/PVA coated samples were heated at 60 °C using the hot plate and under N2 ambient.

The absorbance spectra were acquired using a UV–vis–near-infrared spectrophotometer (Carry-5000, Agilent Technologies). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired with a PL spectrophotometer (Darsa, PSI Trading Co. Ltd.) using excitation λ in the range of 360–440 nm. The atomic structural characteristics were studied by HRTEM (HF-3300/NB5000/S-4800, Hitachi).

The details of the solar cell fabrication process are provided elsewhere [35]. In brief, a three-stage co-evaporation approach was used to create a ∼3 μm CIGS layer on the Mo layer coated on soda lime glass substrate. A p-n junction was subsequently created on the p-type CIGS layer by forming an n-type CdS buffer layer of thickness (50 nm) using a chemical bath deposition technique. By utilizing DC magnetron sputtering, thin films of intrinsic ZnO (i-ZnO) and Al-doped ZnO (ZnO:Al) with thicknesses of 50 nm and 340 nm, respectively, were deposited. Ultimately, a 50 nm Ni layer and a 1 μm Al layer were formed as the front contacts by e-beam evaporation. After developing CIGS PV cells, the polymer (polyvinyl alcohol, or PVA) composite and synthesized NGQDs were used to cover the cells. With a source meter (Keithley 2400), the J-V characteristics (capture area ∼0.43 cm2) were measured under 1-sun AM1.5G illumination. A Si reference cell was used to calibrate the illumination intensity (PV Measurements Incorporated). Every measurement was done at 25 °C. The CIGS PV cell with a PVA layer serves as the control cell. Nevertheless, the NGQD/PVA composite layer prepared at 3000 (Cell #1), 4000 (Cell #2), and 5000 (Cell #3) rpm was applied to CIGS PV cells.

The atomic force microscopy images show that the height of the NGQDs is 1–3 nm (Fig. 2 ). The process of changing an NGQD's surface or structure by adding various functional groups or chemical moieties is referred to as functionalization. Numerous attributes, such as size, morphology, and surface features, may be impacted by this alteration. In this instance, the statement implies that the greater observed height of the NGQDs in the AFM image may be caused by the functionalization of GQDs.

The color of the GQDs solution in white and UV lights (365 nm) is brown and cyan, respectively (Fig. 3 a). Fig. 3b shows the spectrum of ultraviolet–visible absorption of the NGQDs, which is associated with four different absorption bands. The deconvolution of the absorption band revealed four sub-bands, each with a peak at 224, 279, 316, and 354 nm (equivalent to energies of 5.54, 4.44, 3.92, and 3.50 eV). This deconvolution is very appropriate to work as LDC in CIGS PV cells because it has produced a robust and wide absorption band at shorter λ than 500 nm. The π → π* transition of CC is generally attributed to the band in UV (<250 nm) region and the π → π* shift of CO or CN due to absorption in the UV–vis region (250 < λ < 500 nm) [35,45].

The PL excitation spectra of the NGQDs/PVA composite are illustrated in Fig. 4 a. The PL excitation spectra are obtained for the emission λ of 500, 510 and 550 nm, which offer excitation peaks at 410, 410, and 415 nm, respectively. The highest excitation peak intensity is obtained for the emission λ of 500 nm. This indicates that the most suitable excitation wavelength is 410 nm. At this excitation λ, the maximum number of photons can be emitted. Furthermore, the PL emission spectra are obtained for a wide excitation λ in the range of 360–440 nm (Fig. 4b). The emission peak position is rising (from 483 to 510 nm) with the increase in excitation λ (Fig. 4c). An analogous result has been discovered earlier in the literatures [46,47]. N doping and the presence of defects on the surface and edges cause an increase in localized energy levels (trap states) within the bandgap, capturing excited electrons and releasing electrons with a longer emission wavelength. As the excitation wavelength increases, trap states become more occupied, contributing to the red-shifted PL peak [47]. The whole emission range is much appropriate for a competent LDC for CIGS PV cells. The highest PL peak intensity is detected for the excitation λ of 410 nm (Fig. 4d). The highest emission peak should be > 450 nm to achieve better performance of the LDC for CIGS PV cells.

The PL decay spectra are obtained to examine the downconversion efficiency of LDC. The PL decay spectra for various emissions in the λ range of 400–620 nm is shown in Fig. 5 a. The PL decay data was fitted using a double exponential model, which is represented by Eq. (1)
(1)

P L
=
A 1
.
e
(
− t
τ 1
)
+
A 2
.
e
(
− t
τ 2
)
Where τ1 and τ2 are the fast and slow PL decay lifetimes, respectively. A1 and A2 are the constants. The values of the fitting parameters are listed in Table 1 . The dependency of τ1 and τ2 are illustrated in Fig. 5b. It can be seen that the value of τ2 is maximum for the excitation λ of 420 and 600 nm. Furthermore, the average value of PL decay lifetime (τem) is calculated by Eq. (2)
(2)
τ
e m
=
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
A 1
+
A 2
Fig. 5c shows the value of τem, indicating that the average lifetime is over 30 ns for the emission λ of 440–600 nm. These results revealed that the NGQDs/PVA is an efficient LDC for CIGS PV cells.

The NGQDs/PVA composite solution was prepared by adding PVA in DI water and then NGQDs were added (Fig. 1b). The solution is used to coat the LDC followed by natural dry in air ambient. The PVA layer without NGQDs is used as a reference layer. The LDC coated on glass substrates are demonstrated in Fig. 6 . The PVA layer coated on glass substrate is transparent in white light (top left of Fig. 6). However, NGQDs/PVA composite layer shows a light brown color in white light (bottom left of Fig. 6). By illumination of NGQDs/PVA composite LDC layer emits light of cyan color (bottom right of Fig. 6). However, no light emission is observed in the PVA layer coated glass substrate (top right of Fig. 6).

The process was optimized by controlling the spin speed for the formation of the PVA-NGQD layer. The transmittance at various spin speeds is shown in Fig. 7 . The PVA-NGQD layers are optimized by varying spin speeds from 1000 to 7000 rpm. The layer obtained at rpm speeds of 3000, 4000, and 5000 shows an improved absorption in the low wavelength region. However, it is highly transparent in the higher wavelength region. Thus, the LDC layers prepared at 3000, 4000, and 5000 rpm are more appropriate and are applied to the CIGS PV cells.

Fig. 8 shows the structure and mechanism of the LDC-based CIGS PV cell. The LDC layer is implanted onto the top of the CIGS PV cells. The light of long-λ (>500 nm) can transmit from the top layers, which include LDC, ZnO window, and CdS buffer layers. Thus, there is no loss in the long-λ region. However, the light of short-λ (<500 nm) is first absorbed in the LDC layer, which re-emits the photons of higher wavelength (lower energy). The emitted light can pass the window and buffer layers without any loss. Thus, the light of short-λ is also utilized, which improves the J
sc .

The J-V characteristics of the PVA-NGQD cells (Cell #1, Cell #2, and Cell #3) compared with the control cell (Fig. 9 ). The performance parameters are determined from the J-V characteristics. The values of J
sc , V
oc , FF, and η are illustrated in Fig. 10 . Among the three cells, Cell #2 shows the best performance. The highest J
sc (36.1 mA/cm2), V
oc (675.28 mV), and η (15.46 %) values are obtained for Cell #2. The highest FF (63.64 %) value is obtained for Cell #3. However, the values of J
sc , V
oc , FF, and η control cell are obtained as 33.13 mA/cm2, 664.5 mV, 62.15 %, and 13.68 %, respectively.

The J
sc of the CIGS PV cell is increased with the increasing the spin speed for the deposition. Because, at 4000 rpm, there is high absorption in the low-λ region, however, the LDC layer is highly transparent in the longer-λ region. Thus, the J
sc value is highest for the spin speed of 4000 rpm. Correspondingly, the V
oc , FF, and η values are also improved. For the high spin speed (5000 rpm), the transmittance value of the LDC layer is slightly lowered in the longer-λ region, which offer a lower J
sc value (35.31 mA/cm2). However, Cell #3 deliver slightly higher FF value (63.64 %) than Cell #2 (63.41 %). Thus, overall η of the Cell #2 is highest among all the CIGS PV cell (champion cell).

The statistical performance parameters values show the performance enhancement was due to the enhancement in J
sc (Fig. 11 ). The champion cell shows an enhancement in J
sc , V
oc , FF, and η are 8.95, 1.62, 2.03, and 11.51 %, respectively (Fig. 12 ).

The analytical PV cell parameters are determined using a previously developed method [48,49]. This method uses short circuit (SC), open circuit (OC), and maximum power point (MPP). In brief, J
sc , V
oc , current density (J
m ), and voltage (V
m ) at MPP values along with the inverse of the slopes at SC (R
sc ) and OC (R
oc ) are used as input parameters in eqs (1)–(5). The used thermal voltage (V
T ) at temperature (T) is given by = kT/q. where, q is elementary charge and k is Boltzmann's constant.

)

The performance parameters are representative of the actual performance of the PV cells. The performance parameters are governed by J
ph , R
sh , R
s , n, and J
0 . The J
ph shows a similar trend of the variation as J
sc (Fig. 13 a). This indicates that the highest photocurrent is generated for the LDC layer obtained at 4000 rpm. With the insertion of the LDC layer, the R
sh value is enhanced and the highest value of R
sh (273.22 Ωcm2) is obtained for Cell #2 (Fig. 13b). However, the value of Rs is decreased with the application of the LDC layer. The R
s value obtained for the control, Cell #1, Cell #2, and Cell #3 are 1.44, 1.34, 1.28, and 1.27 Ωcm2, respectively (Fig. 13c). Similarly, the values of n and J
0 are reduced via the application of the LDC layer (Fig. 13d and e). The obtained n values are 2.327, 2.291, 2.042, and 1.952 for the control, Cell #1, Cell #2, and Cell #3, respectively. Correspondingly, the detected values of J
0 are 4.56 × 10−7, 4.14 × 10−7, 8.69 × 10−8, and 4.83 × 10−8 for the control cell, Cell #1, Cell #2, and Cell #3, respectively. Both the resistive (R
sh and R
s ) and recombination (n and J
0 ) losses are abridged via the application of the LDC layer.

Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots were formed using a thermal process that employed graphene oxide and polyethyleneimine as a nitrogen source. The NGQDs and PVA were then combined to create polymer and NGQD composite layers. The NGQDs/PVA composite layers produced at 3000, 4000, and 5000 rpm in particular showed promising features, with good transmittance in the longer wavelength area and effective absorption in the short-λ region. The photocurrent density value was greatest in the composite layers formed at a spin speed of 4000 rpm. In this state, open-circuit voltage, fill factor, and overall efficiency were all increased. As a consequence, Cell #2 outperformed all other CIGS PV cells in terms of total efficiency. The champion cell showed improvements of 8.96 %, 1.62 %, 2.03 %, and 11.51 % in J
sc , V
oc , FF, and η, respectively.

Thamraa Alshahrani: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Firoz Khan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation. Masoud Al-Rasheidi: Data curation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Imran Fareed: Data curation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. M. Abdul Majid: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Jae Hyun Kim: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Saudi Standards, Metrology and Quality Organization (SASO) through the Research Program (Grant No. 3-6-12). The researchers thank for the Saudi Standards, Metrology and Quality Organization (SASO) and Deanship of Scientific Research at Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University to supervise and follow up the project.
